summary: The Computer Fraud and Abuse Act criminalizes violations of website terms of service, even when researchers are investigating algorithmic discrimination for the public good. O'Neil highlights how this law impedes important work like ProPublica's investigation into racist recidivism models. She cites research arguing that discrimination audits, while technically violating ethical guidelines, serve a clear public good and should be permitted. The authors argue that moving discrimination into algorithms has actually protected the wicked by making it harder to detect while simultaneously restricting the audit methods that could expose it. The post advocates for CFAA reform to include auditing exceptions in the public interest.
key_points: The CFAA currently criminalizes terms of service violations even when done for legitimate research purposes
ProPublica's influential recidivism algorithm investigation was likely a technical CFAA violation
Traditional discrimination audits (like sending matched resumes to employers) have always bent ethical guidelines but are accepted as serving the public good
Algorithmic discrimination is harder to detect than face-to-face discrimination, yet the CFAA makes it even harder to audit
The law treats corporate processor time as more precious than the resources wasted in decades of in-person audit studies
money_quotes: The CFAA currently criminalizes violations of terms of services for websites, even when those terms of service are written in a narrow way and the violation is being done for the public good.
The CFAA keeps researchers from understanding how algorithms work.
The movement of unjust face-to-face discrimination into computer algorithms appears to have the net effect of protecting the wicked.

Algorithmic discrimination may be much more opaque and hard to detect than earlier forms of discrimination, while at the same time one important mode of monitoring—the audit study—has been circumscribed.

The CFAA treats computer processor time as and a provider's 'authorization' as far more precious than the minutes researchers have stolen from honest landlords and employers over the last few decades. This appears to be fundamentally misguided.
key_insight: By criminalizing the methods researchers need to audit algorithms for discrimination, the CFAA inadvertently protects algorithmic bias from scrutiny.
